serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Aloo Mirch Rissole consists of 2 to 3 pieces weighing approximately 120 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Potato cutlet / aloo tikki, fried — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Aloo Mirch Rissole is a bread-crumbed, deep-fried spiced potato roll. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for fried potato cutlet / aloo tikki, adjusted upward for the bread-crumb coating contribution to carbohydrate and energy. Values represent a reasonable estimate for this preparation style. No exact entry exists in ICMR-NIN for this specific dish.

gi_source.title: Potato products — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Mirch Rissole. GI estimated at 65 based on analogous fried potato preparations (boiled/fried potato products GI range 60–70) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The bread-crumb coating and deep-frying slow starch digestion slightly relative to plain boiled potato (GI ~78). GL per serving calculated as (65 × 29.5 × 1.20) / 100 = approximately 23.0 g for a 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g) and deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and avoid adding salt or high-sodium chutneys, as the combined sodium load from the rissole and condiments can approach 400–500 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 65) and moderate carbohydrate load (~35 g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict intake to one piece per occasion and pair with a low-GI accompaniment such as a green salad or raita to blunt the postprandial glucose response from the starchy potato and bread-crumb coating.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat from deep-frying (~9.8 g per 100 g) and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions, as the deep-frying process increases total and unsaturated fat content; choosing an air-fried or baked preparation would substantially reduce fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Mirch Rissole is an Indian savoury snack consisting of a spiced mashed-potato filling mixed with finely chopped green chillies and aromatic spices such as cumin, coriander, and garam masala, shaped into oval or round patties, coated in bread crumbs, and deep-fried until golden and crisp. The exterior is crunchy while the interior is soft, fluffy, and mildly spicy. The dish is a popular North Indian street-food and restaurant starter, closely related to aloo tikki and aloo cutlet, and is served in Indian restaurants in Singapore such as Kinara at Boat Quay. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides approximately 218 kcal, 4.2 g protein, 9.8 g fat, and 29.5 g carbohydrate, with moderate sodium (~310 mg) from seasoning. The medium glycemic index (estimated GI 65) means blood glucose rises moderately after consumption, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal, but contains gluten from the bread-crumb coating. It is typically served as an appetiser or snack with mint chutney or tamarind sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in aloo mirch rissole?
Is aloo mirch rissole gluten free?
What spices are in aloo mirch rissole?
Is aloo mirch rissole good for diabetics?
What is the GI of potato rissole?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Aloo Mirch Rissole exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fried potato cutlet data with adjustment for bread-crumb coating. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for fried potato products. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-understood and the adaptation basis is defensible.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN fried potato cutlet / aloo tikki entry; no exact match exists for this specific dish. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero for vegan preparations. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this dish contains no animal-derived ingredients and is not fortified; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN plant-food data. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a vegan preparation containing no animal fats; confirmed zero. GI estimated at 65 from analogous fried potato products in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement available for this dish. GL per serving = (65 × 29.5 × 1.20) / 100 ≈ 23.0 g for a 120 g serving. The food_group has been reclassified from the input value of Vegetables to Snacks, which better reflects the deep-fried, bread-crumbed preparation style of this dish.
